__data__.text:
Edge-Deployed Cooperative MARL for
Real-Time Urban Traffic and Emergency
Signal Control
Doctor of Engineering (DEng) Research Proposal
Vivek Rameshbhai Rabadiya
July 7, 2025
Abstract
Urban traffic congestion and emergency response delays pose persistent challenges to mod-
ern cities. Centralized signal systems often suffer from latency, limited scalability, and
inflexibility in dynamic traffic scenarios. This research proposes a cooperative Multi-
Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework for adaptive traffic signal con-
trol with integrated emergency vehicle prioritization. It targets resource-constrained
edge deployment on NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano devices.
The project leverages a SUMO-based traffic simulator, enriched with Open-
StreetMap data, to evaluate real-time control performance, latency, and scalability. It
further incorporates dynamic V2X emergency communications into the learning process.
Expected outcomes include quantifiable reductions in emergency vehicle delays, improved
overall traffic flow, and practical guidelines for deploying MARL-based systems on edge
AI hardware in smart cities.
1
Background
With rapid urbanization, cities face increasing traffic congestion and delayed emergency
responses [3]. Traditional, centrally controlled traffic systems are often rigid, suffer from
high communication latency, and are ill-equipped to dynamically handle emergencies [1,
2].
Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) has emerged as a promising
solution, offering distributed, cooperative traffic control capabilities [3]. However, most
MARL-based studies fall short in:
• Addressing emergency vehicle prioritization within the learning framework,
• Evaluating real-time deployment on low-power edge devices.
Recent work such as MAAC [4] and EMVLight [5] shows promise in adaptive traffic
control and emergency response, yet these systems are largely simulation-bound and
detached from hardware-level feasibility. Emergency prioritization is often treated as an
external or post-processing step rather than embedded in the learning reward structure.
1

Meanwhile, advancements in edge computing — especially embedded systems like
NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano — offer a practical opportunity for deploying energy-
efficient, low-latency AI models on the edge [2].
Building on prior experience with reinforcement learning for emergency vehicle pre-
emption at the German Aerospace Center (DLR), this research aims to fill these
critical gaps. The proposal focuses on integrating emergency prioritization directly into
the MARL reward function, optimizing for real-time, on-device performance using em-
bedded AI hardware.
2
Aims
• Develop a cooperative MARL framework for adaptive, real-time traffic signal con-
trol.
• Embed emergency vehicle prioritization strategies directly within the MARL re-
ward mechanism.
• Evaluate the feasibility and performance of edge-deployed MARL models on NVIDIA
Jetson Orin Nano.
3
Research Questions
1. Effectiveness: How effectively do cooperative MARL agents prioritize emergency
vehicles under real-time, realistic urban traffic conditions?
2. Efficiency: Can MARL models achieve the necessary inference speed, coordination
accuracy, and energy efficiency on resource-constrained edge platforms like NVIDIA
Jetson Orin Nano?
4
Methodology
Simulation Environment
Build realistic urban scenarios in SUMO using OpenStreetMap data. Introduce emer-
gency vehicle routing and real-time incidents.
Reinforcement Learning Agents
Implement and compare algorithms such as PPO, Actor-Critic, and custom hybrid
MARL models using PyTorch. Explore graph-based coordination, reward shap-
ing, and communication-aware MARL architectures.
Emergency Logic
Simulate Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communications to allow real-time phase ad-
justments by agents. Integrate emergency logic into MARL’s reward structure to priori-
tize response efficiency.
2

Coordination Strategy
Evaluate cooperative MARL strategies against traditional fixed-time and heuristic
baselines.
Edge Deployment
Optimize trained models using ONNX and TensorRT. Deploy and evaluate on Jetson
Orin Nano, measuring:
• Inference latency
• Power consumption
• Memory usage
• Real-time responsiveness
Validation
Perform statistical analysis (ANOVA) to compare MARL performance with baseline
systems.
Analyze emergency response times, queue lengths, throughput, and control
latency.
5
Resources Required
• Hardware: NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano
• Software: SUMO, PyTorch, ONNX, TensorRT
• Data: OpenStreetMap-based traffic maps and synthetic traffic/emergency vehicle
patterns
6
Challenges and Ethics
Ethics: No human or animal subjects are involved.
Challenges:
• Ensuring convergence and stability of MARL training
• Fair and dynamic emergency vehicle prioritization
• Maintaining real-time inference under edge computing constraints
7
Expected Outcomes and Significance
This research will deliver:
• An open-source cooperative MARL framework optimized for real-time edge
deployment
• Benchmarks demonstrating emergency handling gains and latency reduction
• Insights into scalable AI deployment for smart city infrastructure
• Potential publications in AI, smart cities, and intelligent transport systems
3

8
Budget and Funding
Software and data sources are open-source. Hardware requirements are minimal and
self-funded. No external funding is required at this stage.
9
Timetable (3 Years)
Stage
Timeframe
Tasks
Literature Review
Year 1 (Sem 1–2)
Study MARL, edge AI, and emergency traffic con-
trol (e.g., MAAC, EMVLight)
Simulation Setup
Year 1 (Sem 2)
Create SUMO environment, simulate traffic and
emergency scenarios
Model Development
Year 2 (Sem 1)
Implement MARL agents with emergency-aware
logic
Training & Testing
Year 2 (Sem 2)
Train models, evaluate against baseline systems
Edge Deployment
Year 3 (Sem 1)
Optimize and deploy models on Jetson devices,
measure performance
Evaluation & Analysis
Year 3 (Sem 2)
Perform statistical and performance analysis
Thesis Writing
Year 3
Write and submit final thesis
References
[1] Chu, T., Wang, J., Codeca, L., & Li, Z. (2019). Multi-agent deep reinforcement
learning for large-scale traffic signal control. IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Trans-
portation Systems, 21(3), 1086–1095.
[2] Xu, H., et al. (2020). Edge computing-enabled smart cities: A comprehensive survey.
IEEE Internet of Things Journal, 7(5), 4513–4545.
[3] Li, Y., et al. (2022). A review on cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning
methods and applications. Neurocomputing, 489, 52–68.
[4] Wu, X., et al. (2020). MAAC: Multi-Agent Auto Communication for MARL-based
Traffic Signal Control. Sensors, 20(15), 4291.
[5] Li, Y., et al. (2022). EMVLight: A Decentralized MARL Approach for Emergency
Vehicle Traffic Signal Control. arXiv preprint arXiv:2206.13441.
4
